papers.summary_en: How much does investor demand move asset prices? Recent work has argued that demand elasticities are surprisingly low, implying that small shifts in who wants to hold what can cause large price movements. This paper embeds demand-elasticity estimation in a dynamic model of asset pricing and asks what it means for inferring the structure of financial markets. The key insight: once dynamics are handled correctly, demand elasticity becomes a time- and state-dependent object, not a single number. The paper develops identification arguments and illustrates with US stock-market data, showing the dynamic framework reconciles several puzzling empirical findings from the static literature.
papers.data_used: Theoretical dynamic model of asset pricing with investor demand. Illustrative empirical application to US stock-market data.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to MNB financial-stability analysis and to Hungarian pension-fund / insurance-company asset-allocation regulation. The paper's key insight — that demand elasticities move over time — means that static estimates of 'price impact' of forced liquidations (e.g., in a bank resolution or pension reform) can be misleading. Supports dynamic stress-testing frameworks and careful analysis of how forced-trading regulation interacts with market liquidity. Caveat: theoretical/US-data paper; operational Hungarian applications would require domestic-market calibration.

papers.title: Browsing versus Studying: A Pro-market Case for Regulation
papers.abstract: Abstract We identify a competition-policy-based argument for regulating the secondary features of complex or complexly priced products when consumers have limited attention. Limited attention implies that consumers can only “study” a small number of complex products in full, while—by failing to check secondary features—they can superficially “browse” more. Interventions limiting ex post consumer harm through safety regulations, caps on certain fees, or other methods induce consumers to do more or more meaningful browsing, enhancing competition. We show that for a pro-competitive effect to obtain, the regulation must apply to the secondary features, and not to the total price or value of the product. As an auxiliary positive prediction, we establish that because low-value consumers are often more likely to study—and therefore less likely to browse—than high-value consumers, the average price consumers pay can be increasing in the share of low-value consumers. We discuss applications of our insights to health-insurance choice, the European Union’s principle on unfair contract terms, food safety in developing countries, and the shopping behaviour of (and prices paid by) low-income and high-income consumers.
papers.summary_en: When products have complicated pricing (airline tickets, phone plans, financial products), consumers often "browse" many options superficially and "study" few in detail. This paper shows that simple ex-post consumer protections — caps on secondary fees, safety regulations on hidden features — can actually intensify competition. When consumers know that hidden features are less bad, they browse more, which makes price competition tougher. Importantly, the regulation must target the secondary features (hidden costs), not the headline price. A pro-market rationale for specific types of consumer protection, grounded in realistic cognition.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct implications for Hungarian consumer-protection regulation (MNB in finance, NFH in telecoms, GVH in general). The paper supports caps on hidden fees and overdraft charges, penalty-feature regulation, and disclosure standards for secondary features — not as a second-best protection, but as competition-enhancing. Relevant to the ongoing implementation of EU consumer-protection directives. Caveat: the theory assumes a specific cognitive structure; ex-ante effectiveness of any specific Hungarian regulation depends on whether the targeted fees fit the "secondary feature" mould.

papers.title: Steering Fallible Consumers
papers.abstract: Abstract Online intermediaries with information about a consumer’s tendencies often ‘steer’ her toward products she is more likely to purchase. We analyse the welfare implications of this practice for ‘fallible’ consumers, who make statistical and strategic mistakes in evaluating offers. The welfare effects depend on the nature and quality of the intermediary’s information and on properties of the consumer’s mistakes. In particular, steering based on high-quality information about the consumer’s mistakes is typically harmful, sometimes extremely so. We argue that much real-life steering is of this type, raising the scope for a broader regulation of steering practices.
papers.summary_en: Online platforms know a lot about their users and often "steer" them toward certain products. When does this help consumers and when does it hurt? This paper analyses consumer welfare when consumers are "fallible" — they make statistical and strategic mistakes. The key insight: steering based on the platform's superior information about the consumer's mistakes (rather than about product fit) is typically harmful, sometimes extremely so. Since in practice much real-world steering uses exactly this kind of information — i.e., exploits errors rather than corrects them — the paper makes a strong theoretical case for regulation of algorithmic recommendation and personalised pricing.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Highly relevant to Hungary's implementation of the EU Digital Services Act and Digital Markets Act. The paper provides a foundation for GVH and NMHH to differentiate between "helpful" personalisation (recommending a product the consumer would actually like) and "harmful" steering (exploiting a known consumer error). Policy implication: tighter rules on steering that is information-asymmetric against the consumer — e.g. dark patterns, default-setting, price discrimination on known biases. Caveat: the paper is theoretical; empirically distinguishing the two types of steering in real Hungarian online markets is non-trivial and would require access to platform algorithms.

papers.title: Competition and Price Variation when Consumers Are Loss Averse
papers.abstract: We modify the Salop (1979) model of price competition with differentiated products by assuming that consumers are loss averse relative to a reference point given by their recent expectations about the purchase. Consumers' sensitivity to losses in money increases the price responsiveness of demand—and hence the intensity of competition—at higher relative to lower market prices, reducing or eliminating price variation both within and between products. When firms face common stochastic costs, in any symmetric equilibrium the markup is strictly decreasing in cost. Even when firms face different cost distributions, we identify conditions under which a focal-price equilibrium (where firms always charge the same “focal” price) exists, and conditions under which any equilibrium is focal. (JEL D11, D43, D81, L13)
papers.summary_en: Why do many firms charge roughly the same price most of the time, with occasional sales? This paper modifies the classic price-competition model to allow for loss-averse consumers who compare actual prices against their recently-formed expectations. Consumer loss aversion makes demand more responsive to higher prices — intensifying competition at the top of the price distribution. The result: prices cluster at a "focal" price, with very little within-product or across-product variation, even when firms face different costs. A behavioural foundation for the observed price stickiness in many consumer-goods markets.
papers.summary_hu: Miért tartja a legtöbb cég többé-kevésbé azonos szinten az árait, alkalmi akciókkal megszakítva? A tanulmány a klasszikus árverseny-modellt módosítja: a fogyasztók veszteségkerülők, és a tényleges árakat a nemrég kialakult várakozásaikhoz viszonyítják. A veszteségkerülés érzékenyebbé teszi a keresletet a magasabb árakra — élénkítve a versenyt az áreloszlás felső szegmensében. Az eredmény: az árak egy 'fókuszáló' ár köré csoportosulnak, kevés terméken belüli vagy termékek közötti szóródással, még akkor is, ha a cégek eltérő költségekkel néznek szembe. Viselkedés-alapú alapot ad a fogyasztói cikkek piacán megfigyelt árstabilitásnak.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Predictions match observed price-setting in consumer-goods retail.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül. Az előrejelzések illeszkednek a fogyasztói cikkek kiskereskedelmében megfigyelt árazási mintákhoz.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian competition and price-regulation analysis: the paper suggests that the observed lack of price dispersion in some retail markets (FMCG, petrol) is not necessarily evidence of collusion; loss aversion provides a competitive explanation. Relevant to GVH assessments of parallel pricing. Caveat: the model doesn't rule out genuine collusion — it just raises the evidentiary bar for inferring anti-competitive behaviour from pure price parallelism. Careful sectoral analysis still needed.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar versenypolitika és árszabályozás szempontjából: a tanulmány szerint a kiskereskedelmi piacok egy részén (FMCG, üzemanyag) tapasztalt alacsony árszóródás nem feltétlenül összejátszás bizonyítéka; a veszteségkerülés versenyszerű magyarázatot ad. Releváns a GVH párhuzamos árazási értékeléseihez. Korlát: a modell nem zárja ki a valódi összejátszást — csak emeli a versenyellenes magatartás bizonyítási küszöbét a tisztán párhuzamos árazás alapján. A részletes ágazati elemzés továbbra is szükséges.

papers.title: Futile Attempts at Self-Control
papers.journal: Journal of the European Economic Association
papers.abstract: We investigate costly yet futile attempts at self-control when consumption of a harmful product has a binary breakdown/no-breakdown nature and individuals tend to underestimate their need for self-control. Considering time-inconsistent preferences as well as temptation disutility, we show that becoming more sophisticated can decrease welfare and investigate what kind of mistaken beliefs lead to low welfare. With time-inconsistent preferences, being close to perfectly understanding one’s preferences but assigning zero probability to true preferences induces the worst outcome. (JEL: D03, D11, D91) 1.
papers.summary_en: When someone wants to quit smoking, limit drinking, or stop gambling, the pattern is often: they try, fail, and try again. This paper shows that people who partially understand their self-control problem can end up with worse welfare than those who don't understand it at all. The reason: partially sophisticated agents exert costly effort at control that ultimately fails — futile attempts. A little self-awareness is dangerous if it's incomplete. The paper identifies the specific belief patterns that lead to the worst outcomes. A cautionary framework for designing health and consumer-protection interventions around self-control problems.
papers.summary_hu: Amikor valaki le akar szokni a dohányzásról, korlátozni az ivást vagy a szerencsejátékot, a minta gyakran ez: próbálkozik, elbukik, újra próbálkozik. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy azok, akik részben értik az önkontroll-problémájukat, rosszabbul járhatnak, mint azok, akik egyáltalán nem értik. Az ok: a részben kifinomult ágensek költséges erőfeszítéseket tesznek a kontrollra, amelyek végül sikertelenek — hiábavaló próbálkozások. Egy kis önismeret veszélyes, ha hiányos. A tanulmány azonosítja azokat a hitminta-konfigurációkat, amelyek a legrosszabb kimenetelhez vezetnek. Óvatos keret az önkontroll-problémákra épülő egészségügyi és fogyasztóvédelmi beavatkozások tervezéséhez.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül.
papers.policy_relevance: Highly relevant to Hungarian public-health policy (smoking, alcohol, gambling) and consumer-protection in addiction-related markets. Message: "raising awareness" interventions that educate people about their self-control problems can backfire unless they deliver full and accurate information. Supports commitment-device based policies (e.g., mandatory cooling-off periods, betting self-exclusion registers) over awareness-only approaches. Caveat: theoretical; specific design of Hungarian interventions requires empirical research on how much self-awareness is realistic.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Erősen érinti a magyar népegészségügyi politikát (dohányzás, alkohol, szerencsejáték) és a függőséghez kapcsolódó piacok fogyasztóvédelmét. Üzenet: a 'figyelemfelkeltő' beavatkozások, amelyek az önkontroll-problémákra oktatják az embereket, visszafelé sülhetnek el, hacsak nem szállítanak teljes és pontos információt. Az elköteleződési eszközökre épülő politikákat (pl. kötelező hűlési időszakok, fogadás-önkizárási regiszterek) támogatja a tisztán figyelemfelkeltő megközelítések helyett. Korlát: elméleti; a magyar beavatkozások konkrét tervezéséhez empirikus kutatás kell arról, hogy mennyi önismeret reális.

papers.title: Exploiting Naïvete about Self-Control in the Credit Market
papers.abstract: We analyze contract choices, loan-repayment behavior, and welfare in a model of a competitive credit market when borrowers have a taste for immediate gratification. Consistent with many credit cards and subprime mortgages, for most types of nonsophisticated borrowers the baseline repayment terms are cheap, but they are also inefficiently front loaded and delays require paying large penalties. Although credit is for future consumption, nonsophisticated consumers overborrow, pay the penalties, and back load repayment, suffering large welfare losses. Prohibiting large penalties for deferring small amounts of repayment—akin to recent regulations in the US credit-card and mortgage markets—can raise welfare. (JEL D14, D18, D49, D86)
papers.summary_en: Credit cards and subprime mortgages often have cheap baseline terms but hefty penalties for late payments or missed deadlines. Why is this the equilibrium contract? This paper models a competitive credit market where some borrowers are "naive" about their own self-control problems. The prediction is stark: contracts offered to naive borrowers look exactly like observed real-world products — cheap front-loaded baseline rates combined with harsh penalties. Naive borrowers overborrow, get hit with penalties, and end up worse off. The paper shows that regulation capping the size of penalties for small delays (similar to recent US credit-card and mortgage reforms) can raise welfare.
papers.summary_hu: A hitelkártyák és másodlagos jelzáloghitelek gyakran olcsó alapfeltételekkel és súlyos büntetésekkel rendelkeznek a késedelmes fizetés vagy elmulasztott határidő esetén. Miért ez az egyensúlyi szerződés? A tanulmány versenyző hitelpiacot modellez, ahol egyes hitelfelvevők 'naivak' a saját önkontroll-problémájukkal kapcsolatban. Az előrejelzés egyértelmű: a naiv hitelfelvevőknek kínált szerződések pontosan úgy néznek ki, mint a megfigyelt valós termékek — olcsó, előre felhalmozott alapkamat súlyos büntetésekkel kombinálva. A naiv hitelfelvevők túlhitelezik magukat, megkapják a büntetéseket, és rosszabbul járnak. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a kis késedelemekre kiszabható büntetések korlátozása (hasonló a közelmúltbeli amerikai hitelkártya- és jelzáloghitel-reformokhoz) jólétet növelhet.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Predictions are illustrated against features of US credit-card and subprime-mortgage contracts.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül. Az előrejelzéseket az amerikai hitelkártya- és másodlagos-jelzálog-szerződések tulajdonságaihoz illusztrálják.
papers.policy_relevance: For the Hungarian retail-credit market (consumer lending, mortgage lending) and MNB consumer protection: the paper provides a welfare rationale for caps on penalty fees, late-payment surcharges, and deferred-interest traps. Particularly relevant to the Hungarian mortgage history (FX-mortgage crisis) and to ongoing supervision of retail consumer credit. Caveat: the evidence is US-based; Hungarian contract structures differ, and the behavioural prevalence of self-control problems in Hungarian consumer-credit populations should be measured empirically before calibrating caps.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar lakossági hitelpiac (fogyasztási hitel, jelzálog) és az MNB fogyasztóvédelme szempontjából: a tanulmány jóléti indoklást ad a büntetődíjak, késedelmi pótdíjak és halasztott-kamat csapdák korlátozására. Különösen releváns a magyar deviza-jelzáloghitel-válság előzményeit és a folyamatos lakossági hitelfelügyeletet illetően. Korlát: a bizonyítékok amerikaiak; a magyar szerződésstruktúrák eltérőek, és a magyar hitelfelvevő-populáció önkontroll-problémáinak gyakoriságát empirikusan kellene mérni a korlátok kalibrálása előtt.

papers.title: Regular prices and sales
papers.abstract: It is widely known that loss aversion leads individuals to dislike risk, and as has been argued by many researchers, in many instances this creates an incentive for firms to shield consumers and employees against economic risks. Complementing previous research, we show that consumer loss aversion can also have the opposite effect: it can lead a firm to optimally introduce risk into an otherwise deterministic environment. We consider a profit-maximizing monopolist selling to a loss-averse consumer, where (following \\citeasnoun{koszegirabin}) we assume that the consumer's reference point is her recent rational expectations about the purchase. We establish that for any degree of consumer loss aversion, the monopolist's optimal price distribution consists of low and variable ``sale'' prices and a high and atomic ``regular'' price. Realizing that she will buy at the sale prices and hence that she will purchase with positive probability, the consumer chooses to avoid the painful uncertainty in whether she will get the product by buying also at the regular price. This pricing pattern is consistent with several recently documented facts regarding retailer pricing. We show that market power is crucial for this result: when firms compete ex ante for consumers, they choose deterministic prices.
papers.summary_en: Supermarkets typically have a stable "regular price" with occasional "sales" at lower prices. Why? This paper offers a behavioural explanation. A monopolist selling to loss-averse consumers (with rational-expectations reference points) finds it optimal to deliberately introduce price volatility. The optimal price distribution has low, variable sale prices and a high, atomic "regular" price. The consumer anticipates buying at sale prices, lowering her reference point; the monopolist exploits the flexibility. The paper shows that loss aversion — typically thought to make firms shield consumers from risk — can lead firms to introduce risk instead.
papers.summary_hu: A szupermarketek jellemzően stabil 'rendes árat' tartanak alkalmi 'akciókkal' alacsonyabb áron. Miért? A tanulmány viselkedési magyarázatot kínál. Egy monopolista, aki veszteségkerülő fogyasztóknak ad el (racionális várakozási referenciaponttal), úgy találja, hogy érdemes szándékosan árvolatilitást bevezetni. Az optimális áreloszlás alacsony, változó akciós árakból és egy magas, atomi 'rendes árból' áll. A fogyasztó arra számít, hogy akciósan vesz, ezzel csökkenti a referenciaponját; a monopolista kihasználja a rugalmasságot. A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a veszteségkerülés — amelyről általában azt gondolják, hogy a cégeket a fogyasztók kockázattól való megóvására készteti — éppen ellenkezőleg, kockázat bevezetésére sarkallhatja őket.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Predictions match stylised facts of retail price-setting.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül. Az előrejelzések illeszkednek a kiskereskedelmi árazás stilizált tényeihez.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian retail-sector regulation and the CPI-measurement challenge (KSH): the paper explains why observed prices look like "regular" and "sale" two-point distributions, with implications for inflation measurement and price-comparison regulation. Relevant to GVH's sense of normal versus anti-competitive pricing. Caveat: the model is stylised; real retail pricing also reflects many other factors (inventory costs, supplier pressure, advertising).
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar kiskereskedelmi szabályozás és a CPI-mérés (KSH) szempontjából: a tanulmány megmagyarázza, miért tűnnek a megfigyelt árak 'rendes' és 'akciós' kétpontú eloszlásnak, ami az inflációmérésre és az árösszehasonlítási szabályozásra is hatással van. Releváns a GVH normális vs versenyellenes árazás megítélésében. Korlát: a modell stilizált; a valós kiskereskedelmi árazás sok más tényezőt is tükröz (készletköltségek, beszállítói nyomás, reklám).

papers.title: On the Welfare Costs of Naiveté in the US Credit-Card Market
Naïveté-Based Discrimination*
papers.abstract: Abstract We initiate the study of naïveté-based discrimination, the practice of conditioning offers on external information about consumers’ naïveté. Knowing that a consumer is naive increases a monopolistic or competitive firm's willingness to generate inefficiency to exploit the consumer's mistakes, so naïveté-based discrimination is not Pareto-improving, can be Pareto-damaging, and often lowers total welfare when classical preference-based discrimination does not. Moreover, the effect on total welfare depends on a hitherto unemphasized market feature: the extent to which the exploitation of naive consumers distorts trade with different types of consumers. If the distortion is homogeneous across naive and sophisticated consumers, then under an arguably weak and empirically testable condition, naïveté-based discrimination lowers total welfare. In contrast, if the distortion arises only for trades with sophisticated consumers, then perfect naïveté-based discrimination maximizes social welfare, although imperfect discrimination often lowers welfare. If the distortion arises only for trades with naive consumers, then naïveté-based discrimination has no effect on welfare. We identify applications for each of these cases. In our primary example, a credit market with present-biased borrowers, firms lend more than is socially optimal to increase the amount of interest naive borrowers unexpectedly pay, creating a homogeneous distortion. The condition for naïveté-based discrimination to lower welfare is then weaker than prudence.
papers.summary_en: Firms sometimes know which customers are "naive" — prone to underestimating fees, penalties, or add-on costs. This paper studies what happens when firms condition their offers on this information. The result: naïveté-based discrimination is never Pareto-improving, and often lowers total welfare, even in cases where ordinary (preference-based) price discrimination would be harmless. The reason is that firms have a stronger incentive to engineer inefficiency to exploit naive consumers than to simply extract surplus. The paper formalises a distinct welfare concern for the regulation of behaviourally-sensitive markets like credit, mortgages, and overdraft fees.
papers.summary_hu: A cégek néha tudják, mely vásárlóik 'naivak' — hajlamosak alulbecsülni díjakat, büntetéseket vagy kiegészítő költségeket. A tanulmány azt vizsgálja, mi történik, ha a cégek erre az információra építve adnak ajánlatokat. Az eredmény: a naivitás-alapú diszkrimináció soha nem Pareto-javító, és gyakran csökkenti az összjólétet, még olyan esetekben is, amikor a hagyományos (preferencia-alapú) árdiszkrimináció ártalmatlan lenne. Ennek oka, hogy a cégeknek nagyobb ösztönzőjük van hatékonyság-rontásra a naiv fogyasztók kihasználása érdekében, mint pusztán a többlet kivonására. A tanulmány külön jóléti aggályt formalizál a viselkedés-érzékeny piacok (hitel, jelzálog, folyószámlahitel-díjak) szabályozása szempontjából.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül.
papers.policy_relevance: For MNB and GVH in Hungary, the paper provides a clean welfare rationale for consumer-protection rules targeted specifically at exploitative-pricing practices (hidden fees, punitive penalties, deferred charges) — as distinct from general price-discrimination regulation. Particularly relevant for consumer credit, payment-card regulation, and overdraft/ATM-fee rules. Caveat: the paper is theoretical; identifying "naïveté-based discrimination" empirically in Hungarian retail-finance or credit-card markets requires detailed product-design and consumer-vulnerability data not typically available in aggregate statistics.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Az MNB és a GVH számára Magyarországon a tanulmány tiszta jóléti indoklást ad a kihasználó árazási gyakorlatokra (rejtett díjak, büntető pótdíjak, halasztott terhek) konkrétan célzó fogyasztóvédelmi szabályokra — szemben az általános árdiszkriminációs szabályozással. Különösen releváns a fogyasztási hitelekre, a fizetési-kártya szabályozásra és a folyószámlahitel/ATM-díj szabályokra. Korlát: a tanulmány elméleti; a 'naivitás-alapú diszkrimináció' empirikus azonosítása a magyar lakossági pénzügyi vagy hitelkártyapiacokon olyan termék-tervezési és fogyasztói-sebezhetőségi adatokat igényel, amelyek aggregált statisztikákban általában nem érhetők el.

papers.title: Behavioral Industrial Organization
Exploitative Innovation
papers.abstract: We analyze innovation incentives when firms can invest either in increasing the product's value (value-increasing innovation) or in increasing the hidden prices they collect from naive consumers (exploitative innovation). We show that if firms cannot return all profits from hidden prices by lowering transparent prices, innovation incentives are often stronger for exploitative than for value-increasing innovations, and are strong even for non-appropriable innovations. These results help explain why firms in the financial industry (e.g., credit-card issuers) have been willing to make innovations others could easily copy, and why these innovations often seem to have included exploitative features. (JEL D21, G21, L11, L25, O31)
papers.summary_en: Why do firms invest in financial 'innovations' that look like they exploit consumers rather than creating value? Heidhues, Köszegi and Murooka show that when firms can collect hidden prices from naive consumers — fees, penalties, deferred charges — innovation incentives often favour exploitative innovation over value-increasing innovation. Their key result: if firms cannot fully refund hidden-price profits via lower transparent prices, exploitative innovation incentives are stronger, and remain strong even for non-appropriable innovations that competitors can copy. The model explains why the financial industry (credit-card issuers, mortgage lenders) made copyable 'innovations' that nonetheless looked exploitative — and why these innovations were profitable to the originator anyway.
papers.summary_hu: Miért fektetnek a cégek olyan pénzügyi 'innovációba', amely inkább kihasználja a fogyasztókat, mint értéket teremt? Heidhues, Köszegi és Murooka megmutatja, hogy amikor a cégek rejtett árakat — díjakat, büntetéseket, halasztott terheket — vehetnek el a naiv fogyasztóktól, az innovációs ösztönzők gyakran a kihasználó innováció felé billennek a valós értéket teremtő innovációval szemben. Kulcseredmény: ha a cégek nem tudják teljes mértékben visszaadni a rejtett-ár profitokat alacsonyabb transzparens árakkal, akkor a kihasználó innováció ösztönzői erősebbek, és erősek maradnak a nem-kisajátítható innovációkra is, amelyeket a versenytársak könnyen másolhatnak. A modell megmagyarázza, miért tett a pénzügyi szektor (hitelkártya-kibocsátók, jelzálog-hitelezők) másolhat 'innovációkat', amelyek mégis kihasználónak tűntek — és miért voltak ezek az innovációk az kibocsátó számára nyereségesek.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Industrial-organization model of innovation incentives with naive consumers and hidden pricing.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül. Iparági szervezet modellje az innovációs ösztönzőkre naiv fogyasztókkal és rejtett árazással.
papers.policy_relevance: Direct relevance to MNB consumer-protection regulation in retail finance. The paper provides a theoretical foundation for distinguishing 'good innovation' (transparent, value-creating) from 'bad innovation' (hidden-fee-extracting), and supports proactive regulation of new financial products before they spread. Useful for MNB and Pénzügyminisztérium consumer-credit and payment-product oversight.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlenül érinti az MNB lakossági-pénzügyi fogyasztóvédelmi szabályozását. A tanulmány elméleti alapot ad a 'jó innováció' (transzparens, értékteremtő) és a 'rossz innováció' (rejtett-díj-kivonó) megkülönböztetéséhez, és támogatja az új pénzügyi termékek proaktív szabályozását, mielőtt elterjednek. Hasznos az MNB és a Pénzügyminisztérium lakossági-hitel és fizetési-termék felügyeletéhez.

papers.title: Inferior Products and Profitable Deception
papers.abstract: We analyse conditions facilitating profitable deception in a simple model of a competitive retail market. Firms selling homogenous products set anticipated prices that consumers understand and additional prices that naive consumers ignore unless revealed to them by a firm, where we assume that there is a binding floor on the anticipated prices. Our main results establish that "bad" products (those with lower social surplus than an alternative) tend to be more reliably profitable than "good" products. Specifically, (1) in a market with a single socially valuable product and sufficiently many firms, a deceptive equilibrium-in which firms hide additional prices-does not exist and firms make zero profits. But perversely, (2) if the product is socially wasteful, then a profitable deceptive equilibrium always exists. Furthermore, (3) in a market with multiple products, since a superior product both diverts sophisticated consumers and renders an inferior product socially wasteful in comparison, it guarantees that firms can profitably sell the inferior product by deceiving consumers. We apply our framework to the mutual fund and credit card markets, arguing that it explains a number of empirical findings regarding these industries.
papers.summary_en: Some products are "inferior" — lower social surplus than a better alternative. Are they more profitable than good products? This paper says yes, counter-intuitively. In a model where firms can "hide" additional fees that naive consumers miss, good products can't sustain deceptive pricing when there are enough firms: competition drives profits to zero. But for inferior/wasteful products, a profitable deceptive equilibrium always exists. When good and bad products coexist, the bad one can crowd out the good by relying on hidden charges. A sharp result with implications for consumer-protection design.
papers.summary_hu: Egyes termékek 'rosszabbak' — kisebb társadalmi hozadékot adnak, mint a jobb alternatíva. Profitabilisabbak-e ennek ellenére, mint a jó termékek? A tanulmány szerint igen, ellenintuitív módon. Egy olyan modellben, ahol a cégek 'eltakarhatnak' kiegészítő díjakat, amelyeket a naiv fogyasztók nem vesznek észre, a jó termékek nem tudnak fenntartani megtévesztő árazást elég cég jelenléte mellett: a verseny nullára szorítja a profitot. A rosszabb / pazarló termékek esetében viszont mindig létezik profitábilis megtévesztő egyensúly. Ha jó és rossz termékek együtt léteznek, a rossz kiszoríthatja a jót a rejtett terhek alkalmazásával. Éles eredmény, fontos következményekkel a fogyasztóvédelmi tervezéshez.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to MNB consumer protection and GVH competition policy. The paper supports tighter regulation of hidden fees and unfair-product rules, particularly in retail credit and consumer contracts, because bad products thrive in deceptive equilibria. Relevant to transposing EU Consumer Rights Directive and to the design of mandatory-disclosure rules that make hidden charges visible. Caveat: theoretical; identifying which real-world products fall into the "socially wasteful" category requires case-by-case assessment.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Közvetlenül érinti az MNB fogyasztóvédelmét és a GVH versenypolitikáját. A tanulmány a rejtett díjakra és tisztességtelen-termék szabályokra vonatkozó szigorúbb szabályozást támogatja, különösen a lakossági hiteleknél és fogyasztói szerződéseknél, mert a rossz termékek megtévesztő egyensúlyokban virágzanak. Releváns az EU Fogyasztói Jogi Irányelv átültetésében és a kötelező-közlési szabályok tervezésében, amelyek láthatóvá teszik a rejtett terheket. Korlát: elméleti; az, hogy mely valós termékek tartoznak a 'társadalmilag pazarló' kategóriába, esetenkénti értékelést igényel.

papers.title: Unrealistic Expectations and Misguided Learning
papers.abstract: We explore the learning process and behavior of an individual with unrealistically high expectations (overconfidence) when outcomes also depend on an external fundamental that affects the optimal action. Moving beyond existing results in the literature, we show that the agent's beliefs regarding the fundamental converge under weak conditions. Furthermore, we identify a broad class of situations in which “learning” about the fundamental is self‐defeating: it leads the individual systematically away from the correct belief and toward lower performance. Due to his overconfidence, the agent—even if initially correct—becomes too pessimistic about the fundamental. As he adjusts his behavior in response, he lowers outcomes and hence becomes even more pessimistic about the fundamental, perpetuating the misdirected learning. The greater is the loss from choosing a suboptimal action, the further the agent's action ends up from optimal. We partially characterize environments in which self‐defeating learning occurs, and show that the decisionmaker learns to take the optimal action if, and in a sense only if, a specific non‐identifiability condition is satisfied. In contrast to an overconfident agent, an underconfident agent's misdirected learning is self‐limiting and therefore not very harmful. We argue that the decision situations in question are common in economic settings, including delegation, organizational, effort, and public‐policy choices.
papers.summary_en: What happens when an overconfident person tries to learn from experience? This paper shows that learning can be actively self-defeating. Overconfident agents systematically misinterpret outcomes: when things go badly, they blame external factors rather than their own choices, becoming too pessimistic about those factors. They then adjust their behaviour in ways that lower future outcomes and reinforce the misguided belief. The greater the cost of the wrong action, the further astray learning goes. The result is a new mechanism by which overconfident individuals (leaders, investors, entrepreneurs) can get stuck in self-confirming mistakes.
papers.summary_hu: Mi történik, amikor egy túlzottan magabiztos ember tapasztalatból próbál tanulni? A tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a tanulás aktívan önromboló lehet. A túlzottan magabiztos ágensek szisztematikusan félreértelmezik a kimeneteleket: ha valami rosszul megy, külső tényezőket okolnak a saját döntéseik helyett, így túl pesszimistává válnak ezekkel a tényezőkkel kapcsolatban. Ezután úgy módosítják viselkedésüket, hogy az csökkenti a jövőbeli kimeneteket és megerősíti a téves hitet. Minél nagyobb a rossz cselekvés költsége, annál inkább félresiklik a tanulás. Az eredmény új mechanizmus, amely által a túlzottan magabiztos egyének (vezetők, befektetők, vállalkozók) önmagukat megerősítő hibákban ragadhatnak meg.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to any policy area where overconfident decision-makers act on feedback — financial regulation (retail investors), management training, entrepreneurship support. The paper suggests that feedback-based "learning" interventions have limited corrective power when overconfidence is present; more effective to structure decisions so correct information is implied by the action. For Hungary's financial-consumer education and SME-support programmes, this implies designing defaults and limits rather than relying on experience-based learning. Caveat: the model is theoretical, applicability depends on whether and how a given group is overconfident.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Releváns minden olyan policy-területen, ahol a túlzottan magabiztos döntéshozók visszacsatolásra reagálnak — pénzügyi szabályozás (lakossági befektetők), vezetőképzés, vállalkozás-támogatás. A tanulmány szerint a visszacsatolás-alapú 'tanulási' beavatkozások korlátozottan tudnak korrigálni túlzott magabiztosság jelenlétében; hatékonyabb a döntések olyan strukturálása, amelynél a helyes információt maga a cselekvés sugallja. Magyarország pénzügyi-fogyasztói oktatása és KKV-támogatási programjai számára ez azt jelenti, hogy alapértelmezett opciókat és korlátokat érdemes tervezni a tapasztalat-alapú tanulás helyett. Korlát: a modell elméleti, az alkalmazhatóság attól függ, hogy egy adott csoport mennyire és hogyan túlzottan magabiztos.

papers.title: Convergence in models of misspecified learning
papers.abstract: We establish convergence of beliefs and actions in a class of one‐dimensional learning settings in which the agent's model is misspecified, she chooses actions endogenously, and the actions affect how she misinterprets information. Our stochastic‐approximation‐based methods rely on two crucial features: that the state and action spaces are continuous, and that the agent's posterior admits a one‐dimensional summary statistic. Through a basic model with a normal–normal updating structure and a generalization in which the agent's misinterpretation of information can depend on her current beliefs in a flexible way, we show that these features are compatible with a number of specifications of how exactly the agent updates. Applications of our framework include learning by a person who has an incorrect model of a technology she uses or is overconfident about herself, learning by a representative agent who may misunderstand macroeconomic outcomes, and learning by a firm that has an incorrect parametric model of demand.
papers.summary_en: People learn from what they do, but if their model of the world is wrong, the learning itself can go astray. This paper establishes precise convergence results for such "misspecified learning" settings: the agent picks actions, sees outcomes, and updates. Even when the updating rule is correct given her (wrong) model, beliefs converge — often to systematically wrong conclusions. The framework covers examples like overconfident learners and people with incorrect mental models of technology. A technical foundation-building contribution that unifies several previous results in behavioural economics.
papers.summary_hu: Az emberek abból tanulnak, amit csinálnak, de ha rossz a világmodelljük, maga a tanulás is félresiklik. A tanulmány pontos konvergencia-eredményeket bizonyít az ilyen 'félredefiniált tanulási' helyzetekre: az ágens kiválaszt cselekvéseket, látja a kimeneteket és frissíti hiteit. Még akkor is, ha a frissítési szabály saját (téves) modellje szerint helyes, a hitek konvergálnak — gyakran szisztematikusan rossz következtetésekhez. A keret olyan példákat fog át, mint a túlzottan magabiztos tanulók és a technológia helytelen mentális modelljét hordozó emberek. Technikai alapozó hozzájárulás, amely több korábbi viselkedés-közgazdaságtani eredményt egységesít.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány; empirikus adatok nélkül.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational for behavioural-economics models that inform Hungarian policy (consumer protection, financial education, public-health communication). Caveat: highly abstract. Specific Hungarian applications require mapping the model's components (agent's mental model, observable outcomes) to concrete decision contexts.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Alapozó hozzájárulás a magyar policy-t informáló viselkedés-közgazdasági modellekhez (fogyasztóvédelem, pénzügyi képzés, népegészségügyi kommunikáció). Korlát: erősen absztrakt. Konkrét magyar alkalmazások a modell összetevőinek (ágens mentális modellje, megfigyelhető kimenetek) konkrét döntési kontextusokra való leképezését igénylik.

papers.title: The equivalence of the minimal dominant set and the myopic stable set for coalition function form games
papers.abstract: In cooperative games, the coalition structure core is, despite its potential emptiness, one of the most popular solutions. While it is a fundamentally static concept, the consideration of a sequential extension of the underlying dominance correspondence gave rise to a selection of non-empty generalizations. Among these, the payoff-equivalence minimal dominant set and the myopic stable set are defined by a similar set of conditions. We identify some problems with the payoff-equivalence minimal dominant set and propose an appropriate reformulation called the minimal dominant set. We show that replacing asymptotic external stability by sequential weak dominance leaves the myopic stable set unaffected. The myopic stable set is therefore equivalent to the minimal dominant set.
papers.summary_en: Cooperative-game theory has many proposed solutions to coalition formation. This paper clarifies the relationship between two competing ones: the minimal dominant set and the myopic stable set. The paper identifies problems with the payoff-equivalence minimal dominant set, proposes a reformulation, and shows the reformulated version is equivalent to the myopic stable set. A careful technical unification of competing solution concepts — useful for applied researchers choosing among them.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data.
papers.policy_relevance: Foundational cooperative-game-theory contribution. Indirect relevance to Hungarian applications of cooperative game theory in mechanism design (kidney exchange, voting-power analysis). Caveat: highly theoretical; applied payoffs depend on specific policy context.

papers.title: A teszteredmények társadalmi egyenlőtlensége és az általános iskolai szegregáció
The effect of decreased general training on skills and dropout - Evidence from a vocational school reform in Hungary
papers.abstract: The paper studies a unique education reform that decreased the length of secondary-level vocational education from 4 to 3 years, reducing the time spent on general subjects while keeping the time spent on vocational training. We use a difference-in-difference strategy by comparing reformed schools with early adopters before and after the reform. We find that students’ general skills have dropped considerably, but the probability of dropout has decreased, and the probability of getting a secondary qualification has increased. These results suggest that such a reform will have mixed labour market consequences, at least in the short run.
papers.summary_en: Hungary shortened its secondary-level vocational education from 4 to 3 years, cutting time spent on general subjects while keeping vocational training. Was it a good idea? This paper uses a difference-in-differences design comparing reformed schools with early adopters. Findings: students' general skills dropped considerably, but dropout probability decreased and secondary-qualification probability rose. Mixed labour-market implications: shorter programmes get more students across the finish line but with weaker general skills. A clear-eyed empirical evaluation.
papers.data_used: Hungarian administrative data on secondary-school outcomes, skills, and dropouts around the vocational-education reform. Difference-in-differences with early-adopter schools as control.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly actionable for Hungarian vocational-education reform debates (Oktatási Hivatal, EMMI, Ministry of Economy). The paper's trade-off — more completions at cost of weaker general skills — is the core policy choice. Relevant to current adjustments of szakképzés (vocational training) programmes. Caveat: short-run skill and dropout effects; longer-run labour-market effects are still to be measured.

papers.title: A kompetenciaeredmények hatása a munkaerőpiaci sikerességre
papers.abstract: Tanulmányunkban nagymintás adminisztratív paneladatokon mutatjuk meg, hogy az országos kompetenciamérés 10. évfolyamos tesztpontszámai erősen összefüggnek a korai munkaerőpiaci helyzettel. Eredményeink szerint egy szórással jobb matematikai tesztpontszám átlagosan körülbelül 4-5 százalékkal, míg egy szórással nagyobb szövegértési tesztpontszám ezen felül további 1-2 százalékkal növeli a korai munkaerőpiaci kereseteket. A munkanélküliség valószínűségét is jelentősen csökkentheti a jobb kognitív készség: összességében egy szórással nagyobb matematikai tesztpontszám 1,5 százalékponttal csökkenti a munkanélküliségi esélyt, míg a szövegértési tesztpontszám növelése további 0,7 százalékponttal csökkenti. A kognitív készségek a magasabb végzettségűek esetében a keresetekre gyakorolnak nagyobb hatást, míg az alacsonyabb végzettségűek esetében nincs hatással a tesztpontszám a későbbi keresetekre. A munkanélküliség esetében minden végzettségi szinten van hatásuk a tesztpontszámoknak.\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: I26, I24, J01.

papers.title: Tanári eredményesség és tanár–diák összepárosítás az általános iskolákban. Empirikus mintázatok három magyarországi tankerület adatai alapján
papers.abstract: A tanulmány egy új, Magyarországon egyedülálló adatbázisra épít, amely három tankerület nyolcadikos diákjainak kompetenciaeredményeit köti össze matematika- és magyartanáraikkal. A kapcsolás révén tanári eredményességet mérő, úgynevezett hozzáadottérték-modelleket becsülünk, majd ezeket felhasználva vizsgáljuk a tanár–diák párosítás mintázatait, vagyis azt, hogy különböző társadalmi helyzetű tanulók egyenlő eséllyel férnek-e hozzá magas hozzáadott értékű pedagógusokhoz. Azt találjuk, hogy a három tankerületben a tanári hozzáadott érték nagysága összhangban áll a nemzetközi irodalomban találtakkal: az átlagosnál egy szórásegységnyivel eredményesebb tanár az általános iskola utolsó két évében körülbelül 0,2 szórásegységnyivel növeli átlagosan a tanulók kompetenciateszt-­pontszámát. Összességében, illetve iskolák között nem találunk kapcsolatot a diákok társadalmi helyzete és az őket tanító pedagógusok eredményessége között, iskolán belül azonban az átlagosan jobb családi hátterű osztályt magasabb hozzáadott értékű tanár fogja tanítani matematikából. Ez az eredmény felhívja a figyelmet az iskolák tantárgyfelosztási gyakorlatának fontosságára, amellyel csekély mértékben ugyan, de valamelyest lehetne enyhíteni a minőségi oktatáshoz való hozzáférés egyenlőtlenségein.*\nJournal of Economic Literature (JEL) kód: I21, I24.

papers.title: Are separate classrooms inherently unequal? The effect of within-school sorting on the socioeconomic test score gap in Hungary
papers.abstract: This study investigates whether within-school sorting increases socioeconomic test score inequalities. Using universal test score data on 6-th, and 8th grade students in Hungary, we document the extent of within-school sorting in an institutional context where sorting based on ability or prior achievement is rare. We identify sorting schools as schools that systematically assign students with low and high socioeconomic status into different classrooms within the school. Then, exploiting school fixed effects and quasi-exogenous variation in sorting induced by enrollment and class size rules, we show that sorting has a significant and economically meaningful effect on test score inequalities between students from different socioeconomic backgrounds. Sorting harms low-status students, while high-status students gain much less, if anything, from attending sorting schools. We attribute our findings to the within-school reallocation of educational resources and differences in educational practices.
papers.summary_en: Even within the same school, students from advantaged and disadvantaged families often end up in different classrooms. Does this within-school sorting matter? This paper uses universal test-score data for 6th and 8th graders in Hungary. Exploiting school fixed effects and quasi-random variation in sorting induced by enrollment and class-size rules, the authors show that sorting has a significant and economically meaningful effect on socioeconomic test-score gaps. Sorting harms low-status students but helps high-status students little or not at all. A clear empirical case against within-school class-composition segregation.
papers.data_used: Universal-coverage administrative test-score data for Hungarian 6th and 8th graders. Exploits school fixed effects and quasi-exogenous variation from enrollment/class-size rules.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly actionable for Hungarian education policy (Oktatási Hivatal, EMMI). The paper provides evidence for Ministry of Education rules against within-school socioeconomic sorting: regulations that prevent class-composition segregation improve outcomes for disadvantaged students without measurably harming advantaged ones. Particularly relevant to Hungary's Roma-education inclusion efforts. Caveat: the effect is identified at a specific grade; long-run impacts into high-school and labour-market outcomes are for future research.

papers.title: Measuring the acceptability of EQ-5D-3L health states for different ages: a new adaptive survey methodology
papers.abstract: The acceptability set of EQ-5D-3L quantifies societal preferences concerning age and disease severity. Its measurement profiles and potential role in health resource allocation needs further exploration.
papers.summary_en: Health-resource allocation benefits from knowing societal preferences over different health states at different ages. The EQ-5D-3L "acceptability set" quantifies these preferences. This paper presents a new adaptive-survey methodology to measure the set more efficiently across demographic groups. The approach allows more precise estimation at lower respondent burden. A methodological contribution useful for health-technology assessment and resource-allocation decisions in healthcare systems.
papers.data_used: Survey data using an adaptive-survey methodology to measure health-state acceptability across age groups.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian health-technology assessment (GYEMSZI, NEAK) and for EU-level health-economic-evaluation methodology. The paper provides a concrete tool for more accurate quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) measurement in Hungarian cost-effectiveness analyses. Relevant to drug-reimbursement decisions and to healthcare priority-setting. Caveat: methodological; operational deployment requires survey-design investment.

papers.title: Immigrant–native pay gap driven by lack of access to high-paying jobs
papers.abstract: Immigrants to high-income countries often face considerable and persistent difficulties in the labour market<sup>1-6</sup>, whereas their native-born children typically experience economic progress<sup>6-9</sup>. However, little is known about the extent to which these immigrant-native earnings differences stem from unequal pay when doing the same work for the same employer versus labour market processes that sort immigrants into lower-paid jobs. Here, using data from nine European and North American countries, we show that the segregation of workers with immigrant backgrounds into lower-paying jobs accounts for about three-quarters of overall immigrant-native earnings differences. Although within-job pay inequality remains notable for immigrants in several countries, our results demonstrate that unequal access to higher-paying jobs is the primary driver of the immigrant-native pay gap across a range of institutionally and demographically diverse contexts. These findings highlight the importance of policies aimed at reducing between-job segregation, such as language training<sup>10-13</sup>, job training<sup>13-15</sup>, job search assistance programmes<sup>13,15</sup>, improving access to domestic education<sup>13,16,17</sup>, recognizing foreign qualifications<sup>18,19</sup>, and settlement programmes aimed at enhancing access to job-relevant information and networks<sup>13,20,21</sup>. Policies that target employer bias in hiring and promotion decisions are also likely to be effective, whereas measures aimed at ensuring equal pay for equal work may have more limited scope for further progress in closing the immigrant-native pay gap<sup>22-28</sup>.
papers.summary_en: Why do immigrants earn less than natives in rich countries? This paper uses linked employer-employee data from nine European and North American countries to separate two channels: (a) unequal pay for the same work at the same employer, and (b) immigrants being sorted into lower-paying jobs. The striking result: job sorting accounts for about three-quarters of the immigrant-native earnings gap. Within-job pay inequality exists and matters, but unequal access to higher-paying employers and positions is the dominant driver. Policy implications shift accordingly: addressing hiring and promotion barriers matters more than equal-pay enforcement.
papers.data_used: Linked employer-employee administrative data from 9 countries (including Hungary, via boza-istvan). Covers 2 billion+ job-years nested within 50 million+ workplace-years. Enables decomposition of earnings gaps into within-job and between-job components.
papers.policy_relevance: Highly relevant for Hungarian integration policy. The paper shows that closing immigrant-native pay gaps requires tackling job-access barriers — credential recognition, language programs, occupation licensing, network-based hiring — more than wage-discrimination enforcement. Relevant to ETDA, BM migration policy, and employer-level diversity efforts. Hungary-specific estimates (via Boza's KRTK work) are embedded in the sample. Caveat: sample is 9 countries; Hungarian context includes specific Roma-employment barriers that are a different case from typical immigrant-native dynamics.

papers.title: Sectoral Technology and Structural Transformation
papers.abstract: We assess how the properties of technology affect structural transformation, i.e., the reallocation of production factors across the broad sectors of agriculture, manufacturing, and services. To this end, we estimate sectoral constant elasticity of substitution (CES) and Cobb-Douglas production functions on postwar US data. We find that differences in technical progress across the three sectors are the dominant force behind structural transformation whereas other differences across sectoral technology are of second-order importance. Our findings imply that Cobb-Douglas sectoral production functions that differ only in technical progress capture the main technological forces behind the postwar US structural transformation. (JEL E16, E25, O33, O47)
papers.summary_en: Why do economies reallocate labour out of agriculture, then manufacturing, and into services as they develop? This paper fits sectoral production functions to postwar US data and finds that differences in technical progress across sectors are the dominant driver of structural transformation — not differences in capital intensity, substitution elasticities, or input requirements. The implication: a simple model with Cobb–Douglas sectoral production functions and sector-specific technical progress captures most of the transformation dynamics. Useful for anyone calibrating multi-sector growth models for developing or transition economies.
papers.summary_hu: Miért allokálják át a gazdaságok a munkát a mezőgazdaságból, majd a feldolgozóiparból, a szolgáltatásokba, ahogy fejlődnek? A tanulmány szektorális termelési függvényeket illeszt háború utáni amerikai adatokra, és azt találja, hogy a szektorok közötti technológiai haladás különbségei a strukturális átalakulás domináns mozgatórugói — nem a tőke-intenzitás, helyettesíthetőségi rugalmasságok vagy input-követelmények különbségei. Következmény: egy egyszerű modell Cobb–Douglas szektor-termelési függvényekkel és szektor-specifikus technológiai haladással a legtöbb átalakulási dinamikát megragadja. Hasznos mindenkinek, aki többsz szektorú növekedési modelleket kalibrál fejlődő vagy átmeneti gazdaságokra.
papers.data_used: US postwar sectoral data, 1947–2010: value added and factor inputs for agriculture, manufacturing, and services from the BEA and BLS; supplemented with input-output tables.
papers.data_used_hu: Amerikai háború utáni szektorális adatok, 1947–2010: hozzáadott érték és tényező-inputok a mezőgazdaságra, feldolgozóiparra és szolgáltatásokra a BEA-tól és BLS-től; input-output táblákkal kiegészítve.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian macro-diagnostic and long-run-growth discussions: the paper supports a view of structural change as technology-driven, which implies Hungarian deindustrialisation since the 1990s is largely natural, not a policy failure. Relevant framing for Ministry of Economy debates on "reindustrialisation" policies — such policies face long-run technology headwinds. Uses US data; Hungarian technology paths differ but the conceptual framework transfers.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar makro-diagnosztikai és hosszú távú növekedési vitákhoz: a tanulmány támogatja a strukturális változás mint technológia-vezérelt nézetet, ami azt implikálja, hogy a magyar deindusztrializáció az 1990-es évek óta nagyrészt természetes, nem szakpolitikai kudarc. Releváns keret a Gazdasági Minisztérium „reindusztrializáció" vitáihoz — az ilyen politikák hosszú távú technológiai ellenszélnek néznek elébe. Amerikai adatot használ; a magyar technológia-pályák különböznek, de a fogalmi keret átvihető.

papers.title: Two Perspectives on Preferences and Structural Transformation
papers.abstract: We assess the empirical importance of changes in income and relative prices for structural transformation in the postwar United States. We explain two natural approaches to the data: sectors may be categories of final expenditure or value added; e.g., the service sector may be the final expenditure on services or the value added from service industries. We estimate preferences for each approach and find that with final expenditure income effects are the dominant force behind structural transformation, whereas with value-added categories price effects are more important. We show how the input-output structure of the United States can reconcile these findings. (JEL E21, L16)
papers.summary_en: Is structural transformation driven by income (as people get richer, they demand relatively more services) or by prices (services become relatively more expensive)? The answer depends on how you slice the data. Using postwar US data, the paper shows that with "final-expenditure" definitions of sectors, income effects dominate; with "value-added" definitions, price effects dominate. The two findings are reconciled by the input–output structure of the economy. The paper is a careful methodological correction to a long-running debate.
papers.summary_hu: A strukturális átalakulást a jövedelem vezérli (ahogy az emberek gazdagabbak lesznek, relatíve többet kérnek szolgáltatásokból), vagy az árak (a szolgáltatások relatíve drágábbak lesznek)? A válasz attól függ, hogyan bontjuk meg az adatokat. Háború utáni amerikai adatokat használva a tanulmány megmutatja, hogy a szektorok „végső-kiadás" meghatározásával a jövedelmi hatások dominálnak; a „hozzáadott érték" meghatározással az ár-hatások dominálnak. A két találat összebékíthető a gazdaság input–output struktúráján át. A tanulmány gondos módszertani javítást ad egy régóta tartó vitához.
papers.data_used: US sectoral data 1947–2010 from BEA national accounts and BLS industry statistics, with final-expenditure and value-added decompositions of agriculture, manufacturing, and services; matched to consumer-price and producer-price indices.
papers.data_used_hu: Amerikai szektorális adatok 1947–2010 a BEA nemzeti számlákból és BLS ipari statisztikákból, végső-kiadás és hozzáadott érték dekompozícióval a mezőgazdaságra, feldolgozóiparra és szolgáltatásokra; fogyasztói-ár és termelői-ár indexekhez illesztve.
papers.policy_relevance: Methodologically important for Hungarian Ministry of Economy staff working on structural-transformation and productivity diagnostics: choice between final-expenditure and value-added sector definitions materially affects the conclusions. Affects how deindustrialisation narratives are told and how policy levers (income-elasticity-sensitive vs relative-price-sensitive) are chosen. US setting; the methodological caveat is universal.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Módszertanilag fontos a magyar Gazdasági Minisztérium strukturális-átalakulási és termelékenységi diagnosztikával foglalkozó munkatársainak: a végső-kiadás vs. hozzáadott érték szektor-meghatározás közötti választás érdemben befolyásolja a következtetéseket. Befolyásolja, hogyan meséljük el a deindusztrializáció narratíváját és hogyan választjuk ki a szakpolitikai eszközöket (jövedelem-rugalmasságra vagy relatív-árra érzékeny). Amerikai környezet; a módszertani figyelmeztetés általános.

papers.title: Growth and Structural Transformation
Structural Change in Investment and Consumption—A Unified Analysis
papers.abstract: Abstract The structural-change literature typically assumes that investment is produced in manufacturing. We establish that this assumption is counterfactual: in the postwar U.S., the share of services value added in investment expenditure has been steadily growing. We develop a new model that features structural change in investment and consumption, characterize its equilibrium properties, and provide empirical support for it. We establish that modelling structural change in investment leads to three novel insights: constant TFP growth in all sectors is inconsistent with the existence of aggregate balanced growth with structural change; the sector with the slowest TFP growth absorbs all resources asymptotically; technical change is endogenously investment-biased.
papers.summary_en: The long-standing story of structural change — labour moving from agriculture to manufacturing to services — has mostly focused on consumption. This paper adds the missing piece: structural change in investment. Using post-war US data, the authors show that services' share of investment expenditure has been steadily growing. They then build and empirically support a unified model of structural change in both consumption and investment. Three new lessons: balanced growth with structural change requires different TFP growth rates across sectors; the slowest-growing sector absorbs all resources asymptotically; and technical change turns out to be endogenously biased toward investment-goods sectors. A conceptually important reframing of growth accounting.
papers.data_used: Post-war US sectoral data: value-added and investment-expenditure shares across goods and services sectors, TFP growth by sector.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian long-run productivity accounting and GDP-growth projections (MNB, PM), the paper reshapes how to think about which sectors drive growth. Services now matter for investment, not just for consumption. Directly relevant to fiscal-space projections and to whether industrial-policy emphasis on manufacturing investment still makes sense. Caveat: the empirical basis is US; Hungarian structural-change paths differ (still more industrial than comparable Western economies), so the quantitative story would need Hungarian calibration before informing specific policy.

papers.title: Determinacy through intertemporal capital adjustment costs
On the stability of the two-sector neoclassical growth model with externalities
papers.journal: Journal of Economic Dynamics and Control

papers.title: Endogenous sector–biased technological change and industrial policy
papers.abstract: We build a model of structural transformation with endogenous sector–biased technological change. We show that if the return to specialization is larger in the goods sector than in the service sector, then the equilibrium has the following properties: aggregate growth is balanced; the service sector receives more innovation but the goods sector experiences more productivity growth; structural transformation takes place from goods to services. Compared to the efficient allocation the laissez–faire equilibrium has too much labor in the goods sector, implying that optimal industrial policy should aim to increase, not decrease, the pace of structural transformation.
papers.summary_en: Models of structural transformation (labor moving from goods to services) usually treat technological change as exogenous. This paper endogenises it. Result: if returns to specialization are larger in goods than in services, equilibrium has balanced aggregate growth; the service sector receives more innovation but the goods sector experiences more productivity growth; labor moves from goods to services. But the laissez-faire equilibrium has too much labor in the goods sector — optimal industrial policy should accelerate structural transformation, not slow it down. A counter-intuitive but clean recommendation.
papers.data_used: Theoretical structural-change model with endogenous sector-biased technology. Comparison to historical sectoral data in developed economies.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian industrial-policy design and long-run growth strategy (Ministry of Economy): the paper supports accelerating structural transformation (faster shift from manufacturing to services) rather than the opposite. Counters nostalgia for manufacturing-heavy industrial policy. Relevant to reindustrialization debates and to EU-level industrial strategy. Caveat: theoretical; the magnitude of the 'too much in goods sector' distortion in Hungarian context should be calibrated.

papers.title: Ruling Out Multiplicity and Indeterminacy: The Role of Heterogeneity
papers.abstract: It is well known that economies of scale that are external to the individual decision makers can lead to self-fulfilling prophecies and the multiplicity or even indeterminacy of equilibrium. We argue that the importance of this source of multiplicity and indeterminacy is overstated in representative agent models, as they ignore the potential stabilizing effect of heterogeneity. We illustrate this in a version of Matsuyama's (1991) two-sector model with increasing returns to scale. Two main results are shown. First, sufficient homogeneity with respect to individual productivity leads to the instability and non-uniqueness of a given stationary state and the indeterminacy of the corresponding stationary state equilibrium. Second, sufficient heterogeneity leads to the global saddle-path stability and the uniqueness of a given stationary state and the global uniqueness of equilibrium.
papers.summary_en: Models with external scale economies famously generate self-fulfilling prophecies — multiple equilibria and indeterminacy where small belief shifts create wildly different outcomes. Herrendorf, Valentinyi and Waldmann argue this is overstated: representative-agent models that ignore individual heterogeneity overdramatize the problem. In a heterogeneous-agent version of Matsuyama's two-sector increasing-returns model, sufficient homogeneity does generate the standard multiplicity and indeterminacy results. But sufficient heterogeneity restores global saddle-path stability and a unique equilibrium. The paper repositions heterogeneity as a stabilising force in growth and structural-change models — an important corrective to the multiplicity-pessimism of the 1990s growth literature.
papers.summary_hu: A külső skálahozadékú modellek híresen önbeteljesítő jóslatokat generálnak — több egyensúlyt és határozatlanságot, ahol kis hitváltozások drámaian eltérő kimenetelekhez vezetnek. Herrendorf, Valentinyi és Waldmann érvelése szerint ez túlzás: a reprezentatív-ágens modellek, amelyek figyelmen kívül hagyják az egyéni heterogenitást, túldramatizálják a problémát. Matsuyama kétszektoros növekvő-hozadékú modelljének heterogén-ágens verziójában a kellő homogenitás valóban generálja a standard többes és határozatlansági eredményeket. De a kellő heterogenitás visszahozza a globális nyeregútpályás stabilitást és az egyértelmű egyensúlyt. A tanulmány a heterogenitást stabilizáló erőként pozícionálja a növekedési és szerkezetváltozási modellekben — fontos korrekció az 1990-es évek növekedési irodalmának többes-pesszimizmusához.
papers.data_used: Theoretical paper. Two-sector increasing-returns growth model with heterogeneous agents; analytical and numerical stability analysis.
papers.data_used_hu: Elméleti tanulmány. Kétszektoros növekvő-hozadékú növekedési modell heterogén ágensekkel; analitikus és numerikus stabilitási elemzés.
papers.policy_relevance: Less directly policy-applicable than empirical papers, but methodologically important: warns Hungarian growth-modelling work (MNB, KRTK) against drawing strong qualitative conclusions from representative-agent models with multiplicity. Suggests that real Hungarian regional disparities are unlikely to be 'lock-in' equilibria of the type the multiplicity literature warned about; structural reform is more tractable than that literature implied.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: Kevésbé közvetlenül szakpolitikailag alkalmazható, mint az empirikus tanulmányok, de módszertanilag fontos: figyelmezteti a magyar növekedés-modellezést (MNB, KRTK), hogy ne vonjon le erős kvalitatív következtetéseket reprezentatív-ágens modellekből többes egyensúllyal. Arra utal, hogy a valós magyar regionális egyenlőtlenségek nem valószínű, hogy 'beszorult' egyensúlyok, ahogy a többes-egyensúlyi irodalom figyelmeztetett; a szerkezeti reform kezelhetőbb, mint amit ez az irodalom sugallt.

papers.title: Cross-border mergers and acquisitions and the role of trade costs
Cycles and chaos in a socialist economy
papers.journal: Journal of Economic Dynamics and Control

papers.title: Early tracking and competition – A recipe for major inequalities in Hungary
Gender differences in preferences of adolescents: Evidence from a large-scale classroom experiment
papers.abstract: In this study, we estimate unadjusted and adjusted gender gaps in time preference, risk attitudes, altruism, trust, trustworthiness, cooperation, and competitiveness using data on 1088 high school students from 53 classes. These data, collected by running incentivized experiments in Hungarian classrooms, are linked to an administrative data source on the students’ standardized test scores, grades and family background. After taking into account class fixed effects, we find that females are significantly more altruistic, but are less present biased, less risk tolerant, less trusting, less trustworthy, and less competitive than males. At the same time we do not observe significant gender differences in patience, time inconsistency and cooperation at the 5% significance level. We also show that most of these initial gender differences do not change even if we control for age, family background, cognitive skills and school grades in a regression framework. We risk over-control when we include the time spent on each task as well as the other preference domains in our regressions, but the gender gap remains significant in social preferences (altruism, trust and trustworthiness), present bias and competition.
papers.summary_en: Where do gender gaps in preferences show up among adolescents? Using incentivized classroom experiments with 1,088 Hungarian high-school students linked to administrative records, this paper measures time, risk, altruism, trust, cooperation, and competitiveness. Adjusting for class fixed effects, females are significantly more altruistic but less present-biased, less risk-tolerant, less trusting, less trustworthy, and less competitive than males. Controlling for age, family background, cognitive skills, and grades does not eliminate these gaps. The paper documents which preference dimensions are gendered at this age — important for understanding how gender gaps emerge later in careers.
papers.summary_hu: Hol mutatkoznak nemi szakadékok a preferenciákban a kamaszok körében? 1 088 magyar középiskolás ösztönzött osztálytermi kísérleteit és adminisztratív rekordokkal összekapcsolva a tanulmány méri az idő-, kockázati-, altruisztikus-, bizalom-, kooperációs- és versengési-preferenciákat. Osztály fixhatásokra kiigazítva a lányok szignifikánsan altruistább, de kevésbé jelenhajlamos, kevésbé kockázattoleráns, kevésbé bizalmas, kevésbé megbízható és kevésbé versengő, mint a fiúk. Az életkor, családi háttér, kognitív képességek és jegyek kontrollálása után sem szűnnek meg ezek a szakadékok. A tanulmány dokumentálja, mely preferencia-dimenziók nemi meghatározottságúak ebben az életkorban — fontos annak megértéséhez, hogyan alakulnak ki a nemi szakadékok később a karrierekben.
papers.data_used: Incentivized classroom experiments with 1,088 high-school students across 53 Hungarian classrooms, linked to administrative records (standardized test scores, grades, family background).
papers.data_used_hu: Ösztönzött osztálytermi kísérletek 1 088 középiskolás diákkal 53 magyar osztályban, adminisztratív rekordokhoz (szabványosított teszt-pontszámok, jegyek, családi háttér) kapcsolva.
papers.policy_relevance: For Hungarian education policy: the paper provides Hungarian-specific evidence on adolescent gender-preference gaps, which is input to STEM-track-encouragement programmes, career-counselling design, and competitive admission-exam design. The large gender gap in competitiveness at age 15–18 is directly relevant to debates about how competitive vs non-competitive school-admission systems amplify or dampen gender effects on educational trajectories.
papers.policy_relevance_hu: A magyar oktatáspolitikához: a tanulmány magyar-specifikus bizonyítékot ad a kamaszkori nemi-preferencia szakadékokról, ami bemenet a STEM-pálya ösztönző programokhoz, pályatanácsadási tervezéshez és a versenyző felvételi vizsga-tervezéshez. A 15–18 éves kori versengésbeli nagy nemi szakadék közvetlenül releváns ahhoz, hogyan erősítik vagy tompítják a versenyző vs. nem-versenyző iskolai-felvételi rendszerek a nemi hatásokat az oktatási pályákon.

papers.title: Per-Shipment Costs and the Lumpiness of International Trade
papers.abstract: Using detailed U.S. and Spanish export data, we document that trade costs of a per-shipment nature are associated with less frequent and larger shipments (i.e., more lumpiness) in international trade. This finding is pervasive across broad product categories, but most apparent for industrial supplies, parts and accessories, and food products.
papers.summary_en: International trade happens in lumps, not smoothly: a firm exports to a country, then pauses, then exports again. This paper shows why. Using detailed US and Spanish customs data, the authors show that when per-shipment costs are higher, shipments are larger but less frequent (more lumpy). The relationship holds across all broad product categories — but is strongest for industrial supplies, parts/accessories, and food products. A clean empirical grounding for an important but underappreciated source of trade costs: the fixed cost of each individual shipment.
papers.data_used: Administrative customs data from US and Spain: shipment-level records of exports with product, destination, weight, and value. Covers the full set of export shipments over multiple years.
papers.policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian trade-facilitation policy (customs, logistics, port infrastructure). The paper supports measures that reduce per-shipment costs (digital customs, border-crossing speed): their effect is to make trade less lumpy and more frequent, which improves buyer flexibility. Specifically relevant to EU customs harmonisation and to ITM infrastructure investments in transport corridors. Caveat: the evidence is from US and Spain; Hungary's role in EU supply chains differs, but the mechanism (per-shipment fixed costs) is general.

papers.title: Administrative barriers to trade
papers.abstract: We build a model of administrative barriers to trade to understand how they affect trade volumes, shipping decisions and welfare. Because administrative costs are incurred with every shipment, exporters have to decide how to break up total trade into individual shipments. Consumers value frequent shipments, because they enable them to consume close to their preferred dates. Hence per-shipment costs create a welfare loss. We derive a gravity equation in our model and show that administrative costs can be expressed as bilateral ad-valorem trade costs. We estimate the ad-valorem equivalent in Spanish shipment-level export data and find it to be large. A 50% reduction in per-shipment costs is equivalent to a 9 percentage point reduction in tariffs. Our model and estimates help explain why policy makers emphasize trade facilitation and why trade within customs unions is larger than trade within free trade areas.
papers.summary_en: Per-shipment administrative costs (customs paperwork, inspections, fees) are a surprisingly important form of trade cost. This paper builds a model showing how they distort trade volumes and shipment patterns. Exporters have to decide shipment frequency; consumers value frequency; so per-shipment costs create welfare loss. The paper derives a gravity equation in the model and shows these costs translate into bilateral ad-valorem trade costs. Using Spanish shipment-level export data, the authors find the ad-valorem equivalent is large: a 50% reduction in per-shipment costs is equivalent to a 9-percentage-point tariff cut. A clean empirical quantification.
papers.data_used: Spanish shipment-level customs export data, including shipment timing, destination, and value.
papers.policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian trade-facilitation policy (NAV customs, ITM) and EU customs-harmonisation debates. The paper's 50%-cut-equals-9pp-tariff equivalence is a powerful argument for digital-customs investment. Relevant to implementation of the EU Union Customs Code. Caveat: Spanish data; Hungarian effects depend on baseline per-shipment costs and industry mix.

papers.title: Winners and Losers of Globalization: Sixteen Challenges for Measurement and Theory
papers.abstract: The goal of this chapter is to summarize the state of the art in research in international trade and global production, and discuss issues relevant to European policymakers. Much of recent research on globalization is primarily empirical, owing to the proliferation of available data. We begin by discussing recent advances in measuring the causes and effects of globalization, and discussing the particular data challenges that have emerged. We then turn to theories of trade and global production, first summarizing the conclusions on which there is a broad consensus in the field.
